item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this one is just awesome. I don't find it creepy as much as it is logical. "Governments using available technology to create weapons? That makes sense." The great part is that suspension of disbelief is kept because of the meticulous details put behind SCP-2195, even though I found it unnecessarily wordy. I recall there being mention of "auditory mechanisms" or something similar, when I think "organs capable of audition" is sufficient.

That being said, it could have used better sectioning. The first part is the description and purpose of SCP-2195; next is the history and success (or failure) of the project; third is a hodgepodge, not particularly coherent; rest are addendums, which are short and sweet, true addendums.

I did find it particularly odd so much was found at a checkpoint. Reproducible steps would be transferred as well as future ideas + motivation, but not on future propaganda campaigns.

But the overall effect I think is quite satisfying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As others have said earlier in this comment thread, the real masterpiece here is the precision and detail used to paint a place that, while superficially similar to our own world in many ways, has diverged enough in their technology and morals to have a campaign to make women give birth to infant bombs a reality. Utterly chilling. +1

Also what the FUCK is that image? What is that even OF?

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I've reinstated the image.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Source image finally got uploaded under appropriate licence.
https://www.flickr.com/photos/187534264@N02/49848281113/in/dateposted-public/
Is this suitable?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the image's original source cannot be found, and no source with it indicates it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

EDIT: The image was created by the original author, and thus is cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is perfect in its own way.
Perfect in making human beings with moral standards sick to the bones.
Perfect enough to be the first skip I have a need to downvote because of all the disqusting images it put into my head, and then I saw the photo…

-1 because even the concept itself is gross to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of the few articles on the site to actually disturb me and make me sick to my stomach. It's improved by many of the most disturbing elements - such as the government forcibly making women carry these things - not actually being anomalous. It's those real elements that get me the hardest - SCP-3000-EX is, to me, the scariest article on the site for that reason.

This was awful in the best way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This had some philosophical implications for me that were more horrifying than the bomb fetuses. The bomb fetuses were horrifying as well. I'm not an easy man to scare; nice work.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I felt that some of the italicized comments from the researchers would have worked better consolidated into a single addendum addressing the apparent cultural differences that contributed to the creation of SCP-2195.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep, this is just plain horrifying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One such act was committed in a maternity welfare center which caused a chain reaction and large-scale destruction.

UPVOTED SO HARDDDDDDDDDDDD HAHAHAHA

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do know how to do quote boxes (once I remind myself how to do all the font changing stuff, because I so rarely comment) but I was on a crappy tablet and it just wasn't working.

Also, yes, you are totally correct. I should have included intersex. My train of thought at the time was that it would be implied but I should have said it. My apologies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. Corrected that part. Gender is a bit of an alien term for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, just for the record, you can make a quote box by using a > and a space in front of the words. The benefit of this is that is shows more distinct quotes and makes it easier to read. Example of a quote box:

quote box

In relation to the actual content of your post, you do realize that male and female aren't the only sexes right? There's also intersex, and doctors usually say when a baby is intersex.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I believe someone may have mentioned this but:

"are living, genetically altered human infants of indeterminate gender"

sex != gender. Male and female would be the sex of the baby based on their chromosomes and genitals; their gender identity however, it seems we never get to find out.

This could also be a quirk of translation. I imagine concepts like this don't translate directly.

"and this object is only a miscarriage of some alternate history branch"

Ahhhh, I see what you did there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's what the author has to reply:
Social and political aspects behind the project are important, but it does have practical value. At the time of writing it was assumed that science developed in a different way in NP-verse: they're far more advanced than us in biotech (i.e. they can mass-produce 2195 relatively cheap) but electronics is comparable to what we had in 1950s. Probably, biotech advancements slowed other areas of tech down by making them redundant. The same advancements caused a lot of trouble sometime ago, so there's an official ban on biotech in NP-verse since then.
Aviation is not really developed and their planes can't carry a lot of bombs. Since they cannot carry more bombs or make bombs with bigger payload, the only way to make them more effective is to make them homing. And electronic targeting systems are beyond imaginable for them.
In our world, during WW2 the only way to mass-produce homing weapons was to use kamikaze pilots. This project's developers went one step further and grew a weapon that has such a pilot right from the start.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh, baby boomers.

Good article. I liked the second addendum personally. As I was reading, I had the same question some of the researchers evidently did: with all the effort that goes into these things, why use them instead of purely mechanical weapons? So it's interesting to think that the motivation behind the project was way more social and political than it was practical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ugh, this just feels deeply wrong in an inane way.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, I really like this article. The baby-bombs are interesting, but the country/reality they come from is where this article really shines. I want to know more about this depressing place, with it's terrible government. The article does drag a bit in places and could be trimmed up, especially in the description of the baby-bombs.

I don't understand why others have complained about the last collapsible, certain complaints anyways. It was a simple experiment to test the validity of the documentation found, and whether or not the process is applicable to humans as we know them. Experimentation into the origins and nature of anomalies is the norm. This is 100% something the Foundation would do, and this sort of treatment towards Class-D is far from unprecedented. The part about the collapsible I don't like is the part about it having "no practical value to the Foundation". I feel like that should be removed. The Foundation does research out the wazoo, but they don't weaponize. So it really shouldn't even be mentioned as a possibility.

Up vote regardless of that one gripe.

item.title: Re: I'm downvoting because I disagree with The Foundation forcibly impregnanting people.
item.content:encoded:
It was done once, to provide valuable insights.

Among other things, it showed that the process indeed applies to humans such as exist in our universe, which for this sort of materials is not exactly a given. Plus, the fact that they showed to have no undocumented features that might make them a valuable resource to the Foundation seems like a trite finding, but that's all hindsight - if it had shown not to be the case, those in charge of the decision would have been lauded.

You are entitled to your opinion, but honestly, I fail to see the Foundation following its own doctrines as "lame and shitty."

item.title: Re: I'm downvoting because I disagree with The Foundation forcibly impregnanting people.
item.content:encoded:
That doesn't change the fact that the Foundation is forcibly impregnating D-class against their will "just to see if it works".

That's kind of a lame and shitty thing to do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Posted some info from the author in The Leak thread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't even-

I can't even-

This article has robbed me of my capacity to even.

How odd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The large amount of details do spoil the surprise a bit early. Some paragraphs, like the one about the "higher will" and the doctor's comments, felt pretty pushy on the main idea of the article. I'm always iffy on medical details, as while they do make an SCP slightly more immersive, I always end up glazing over them.

That all being said, +1. I would love to see one of the documents found as a tale. maybe i'll take a crack at it eventually

item.title: Grammatical stuff, some of it minor.
item.content:encoded:
Hello! Just thought I'd go through a few grammatical and style things. If you disagree for stylistic reasons, feel free to ignore them.

+ show block
– hide block

… although the origin of names and toponyms mentioned within seems to be more English.

This isn't quite as formal as the previous statement about French, and implies nationality rather than language.

1. original documents use a specific philosophical term which, according to the context, should mean predetermination or being destined by a higher force, whose will it is immoral and unnatural for a human being to resist

I think footnotes are usually capitalized and punctured as sentences.

Note: it should be noted that no cases of an illegal abortion were described.

Minor point: I got the intention, but "Note: it should be noted" is slightly awkward. I mean, they are noting it. A different phrase of emphasis might be better.

the need to conceal SCP-2195-1 true origins in order to support the propaganda,

I believe propaganda is a weird Latin plural and doesn't generally use an article, unless referencing something like "the propaganda campaign".

Edit: My mistake: Weird Latin gerund. Still weird and Latin.

A large part of these documents is encrypted, their contents is currently unknown.

"contents are"… although "their contents currently unknown." would also work.

It is assumed that the whole SCP-2195 was composed as fulfillment of said decree.

Probably "whole of SCP-2195". SCP designations are grammatically vague, in my experience.

Completely aside from grammar, I thought the article was excellent. Like others, I thought the collapsible was unnecessary and detracted from the article. I'm not familiar with the Russian site, but that seems like the kind of thing you'd find in a three-digit article, not so much recently. It wasn't enough to really mar the rest of it, which seems like a large amount of work done well by both author and translator. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good body horror. Maybe a tad long, as I skipped a bunch of stuff before the collapsible and don't feel I missed anything. But it's just, damn, who the hell would come up with this kind of thing in-universe? Classic feel to this article that I approve of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think if there was more buildup to the exploding fetus thing, instead of getting right to the big reveal and dragging it on for a long time, I would have liked this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Marvelous horror. The effort expended in translation was well spent!

For me, the last line is a key element of the horror.

SCP-2195-1 was declared having no practical value to the Foundation.

Mayyyybe the Foundation only replicated it to establish whether the documentation represented something real. Then again, mayyyybe the Foundation replicated it on the off chance it was as useful as «Notre Patrie» thought it was. I know that rubs a lot of headcanon the wrong way, but that dissonance helps make it work.

Breaking a well established convention (the Foundation doesn't weaponize) is a fine device when a strong piece supports the twist.

item.title: Re: I'm downvoting because I disagree with The Foundation forcibly impregnanting people.
item.content:encoded:
As for the reason for replicating SCP-2195-1, this was not made in order to weaponize the thing. This was made in order to check whether the documents were actually true.

Indeed. To boot, this attitude is reflected in the test notes where it says that the lack of any properties beyond that of a conventional if highly effective bomb gives no reason to carry out more such breeding projects.

Seriously, using D-class in this manner is very much NOT unprecedented, and given the situation the Foundation is in, they can hardly afford for this sort of concerns to get in the way of analysis.

Also:

This is inaccurate. There is already a term for this known as intersex. These babies are intersex.

I'm not sure that's the correct term when a) what's in place of the sex-determining chromosomes doesnt' resemble human allosomes at all, and when the offspring have a decidedly non-human anatomy.

I don't know if this is really accurate.

I don't understand your concern there. Are you complaining about the scientific vocabulary used, or somehow disputing a feature of these things' anatomy? (I think the first, but I'm puzzled)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't even-

I can't even-

This article has robbed me of my capacity to even.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Spontaneous fetus detonations" is not a phrase I expected to read in my lifetime.

item.title: Re: I'm downvoting because I disagree with The Foundation forcibly impregnanting people.
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the corrections. Some of these were implemented (also, changed "sharp" to "acute" as a medical term).
Re illegal abortions: in some countries abortions are illegal. Some underground abortion clinics should exist as a reaction to a demand which cannot be legally satisfied. That world's ethics clearly makes abortions illegal, but no cases of underground abortion were noticed, which seemed odd to the Foundation researches.
As for the reason for replicating SCP-2195-1, this was not made in order to weaponize the thing. This was made in order to check whether the documents were actually true. Turns out they were.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: EDIT: Somehow I missed that last collapsible. I'm not sure why the Foundation would even attempt to try and replicate this. Vote withheld.

item.title: I'm downvoting because I disagree with The Foundation forcibly impregnanting people.
item.content:encoded:
Click to Show full review
Click to hide

SCP-2195-1 are living, genetically altered human infants of indeterminate gender (SCP-2195-1 allosomes are identical, and contain genetic information from both X and Y chromosomes).

This is inaccurate. There is already a term for this known as intersex. These babies are intersex.

which leads to a small dead-end cavity

I don't know if this is really accurate.

According to the documents, SCP-2195-1 have a fetal life of 40 weeks which is normal to humans.

This could be rewritten easier as "SCP-XXXX-1 have a gestation time of 40 weeks."

; arms industry was also about to decline

Inappropriate semicolon use. Would recommend changing it to something like "the arms industry was also declining"

which was ground for euthanasia.

grounds*

An essay of the problem's observations is available.

This is very awkward. Suggest changing to "An essay of observations concerning this issue is available."

Note: it should be noted that no cases of an illegal abortion were described.

I don't understand this note.

SCP-2195-1 was declared having no practical value to the Foundation.

I don't agree with the Foundation forcibly impregnating a woman to see if they could use these bombs for themselves.

We have an ethics committee that I believe would be against forcibly impregnating D-class females with anomalous fetuses for the purposes of weaponizing an SCP. TBH I find that last bit extensively distasteful. The rest of it is also fairly distasteful to me, but can be at least tolerated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really, really good. It perfectly balances explaining as much as it needs to while retaining a lot of mystery. The world-building and explanations of the alternative ethics of the alternate universe are very impressive, as well as the detail on the specimens and the documentations. Of course, the backstory and basic ideas are really horrible, and not just because of the bomb-babies body horror. Congratulations to TheTBAPb, and many thanks to Gene R for translating it for our reader pleasure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really it means the same thing, but Siamese Twin is an older term that's considered non-PC these days. (and also can cause confusion about whether you mean non-conjoined twins of Siamese descent. X3)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Between this and the hole in the budget, I continue being impressed with TheTVAR's efforts.
This article contains both excellent detailed and fairly plausible descriptions of the speculative specimens, and rather creepy and interesting backstory (BTW I wonder now if Notre Patrie exists in the same universe as Alexylva University) explaining their existance.
It was a joy to proofread and joy to upvote.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a bunch of tonal errors and weird wording that I can attribute to this being translated which, unfortunately, I'm not in a position to point out.

That aside, though, this is deliciously, horrifyingly creepy stuff. Massive kudos to the original author for doing body horror right!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, that was a false cognate. Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty sure the correct medical term for twins that are stuck together is "conjoined twins," not "Siamese twins." No other problems that I could see. This is pretty cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another article from the Russian wiki.
Originally autored by theTBAPb, translated by yours truly, proofread by VAElynx. As always, feel free to correct/point out any errors you may find.
